posts.name: Steve Gautier
posts.cooked:
News from Wine Advocate - congrats to William Kelley on the promotion!

Dear Reader,

As someone who counts the first paperback edition of Parker’s Wine Buyer’s Guide among the first wine books I purchased—alongside Tom Stevenson’s Sotheby’s World Wine Encyclopedia and Hugh Johnson’s Pocket Encyclopedia of Wine—it’s with a great sense of privilege and responsibility that I write today as the incoming Editor-in-Chief of Robert Parker Wine Advocate.

For this opportunity, I owe a great deal to Bob himself, who recognized more than two decades ago that the wine world had grown too large for any single person to be an expert in everything, and to my immediate predecessor, Lisa Perrotti-Brown, MW, who recruited me as managing editor nearly five years ago. Both of them were incredibly supportive of my efforts during their time here, and their regular counsel and steady hands will be missed.

Looking ahead, aside from Lisa’s departure, we’ve retained our entire cast of expert reviewers and are actively seeking to add at least one more member to the team. William Kelley, DPhil, has been promoted to Deputy Editor and will take over reviewing responsibilities for Bordeaux, in addition to Burgundy and Champagne. William has been a passionate follower of Bordeaux since his student days at Oxford, with extensive exposure to the great wines of the 1940s, 1950s and 1960s. Now based in France, he’s ideally situated to provide readers with the same boots-on-the-ground coverage of Bordeaux as he’s been delivering on Burgundy since 2017.

I’ll be following a path blazed by Bob for decades, regularly traveling to Napa to review the wines that originally captured my heart. Some of my most formative wine experiences revolve around Napa Valley: my first wine-region vacation in 1990, the first long-form article I penned as a professional wine writer and two invitations—once as a speaker—to the Symposium for Professional Wine Writers.

While I’m looking forward to returning to those roots and rediscovering all that Napa has to offer, I’ll be adding Napa to my already over-filled plate, so you can expect to see some tweaking of tasting responsibilities when we hire a new reviewer. What exactly those will be will depend on who we recruit.

In addition to adding another reviewer, with the extensive backing of the Michelin Group now behind us, in the near term, we expect to revamp our website, develop additional video content and relaunch in-person tasting events when and where public health conditions allow.

William and I are excited to be leading a team of wine critics that’s committed to building on Robert Parker’s legacy as the ultimate consumers’ guide to wine. To truly fulfill that mission, we’d love to hear from you. Our marketing team recently sent out a survey so that we can get a better idea of what exactly you want—if you haven’t already done so, please take the five minutes or less it takes to fill out this survey and press the submit button.

Regardless of these changes and any others that result from your feedback, we know that you value our team’s expertise and independence, and those priorities—the bedrock of Robert Parker’s publication—won’t change. You’ll be able to continue to depend on our reviewers to call things as they see them, giving you the information you need to make your own wine-buying decisions.

Cheers,
Joe Czerwinski

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
William on Bordeaux is huge. Outside of Gilman, who really doesn’t review young Bordeaux or do barrels, there really is no other truly credible professional voice, IMHO. I haven’t followed any of them, not even N. Martin. I have been better served by the folks on this board, many of whom have a great palate for Bordeaux. I’d love to list some names, but invariably I’d miss someone and then feel like crap. I will make one note, if you follow any amateur on Bordeaux, check out Keith Levenberg’s CT notes. They have yet to steer me astray.

With William up, and Black Forest Cake out, I’ll rejoin TWA.

Well, there goes some my rationale for not subscribing to TWA after cancelling in reaction to the questionable ethics of certain TWA writers/staff, who now all look to be former writers/staff (including LPB).

Most importantly, congrats to William Kelley, who is (along with John Gilman) the best wine writer in the biz.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
News from Wine Advocate - congrats to William Kelley on the promotion!

Dear Reader,

As someone who counts the first paperback edition of Parker’s Wine Buyer’s Guide among the first wine books I purchased—alongside Tom Stevenson’s Sotheby’s World Wine Encyclopedia and Hugh Johnson’s Pocket Encyclopedia of Wine—it’s with a great sense of privilege and responsibility that I write today as the incoming Editor-in-Chief of Robert Parker Wine Advocate.

For this opportunity, I owe a great deal to Bob himself, who recognized more than two decades ago that the wine world had grown too large for any single person to be an expert in everything, and to my immediate predecessor, Lisa Perrotti-Brown, MW, who recruited me as managing editor nearly five years ago. Both of them were incredibly supportive of my efforts during their time here, and their regular counsel and steady hands will be missed.

Looking ahead, aside from Lisa’s departure, we’ve retained our entire cast of expert reviewers and are actively seeking to add at least one more member to the team. William Kelley, DPhil, has been promoted to Deputy Editor and will take over reviewing responsibilities for Bordeaux, in addition to Burgundy and Champagne. William has been a passionate follower of Bordeaux since his student days at Oxford, with extensive exposure to the great wines of the 1940s, 1950s and 1960s. Now based in France, he’s ideally situated to provide readers with the same boots-on-the-ground coverage of Bordeaux as he’s been delivering on Burgundy since 2017.

I’ll be following a path blazed by Bob for decades, regularly traveling to Napa to review the wines that originally captured my heart. Some of my most formative wine experiences revolve around Napa Valley: my first wine-region vacation in 1990, the first long-form article I penned as a professional wine writer and two invitations—once as a speaker—to the Symposium for Professional Wine Writers.

While I’m looking forward to returning to those roots and rediscovering all that Napa has to offer, I’ll be adding Napa to my already over-filled plate, so you can expect to see some tweaking of tasting responsibilities when we hire a new reviewer. What exactly those will be will depend on who we recruit.

In addition to adding another reviewer, with the extensive backing of the Michelin Group now behind us, in the near term, we expect to revamp our website, develop additional video content and relaunch in-person tasting events when and where public health conditions allow.

William and I are excited to be leading a team of wine critics that’s committed to building on Robert Parker’s legacy as the ultimate consumers’ guide to wine. To truly fulfill that mission, we’d love to hear from you. Our marketing team recently sent out a survey so that we can get a better idea of what exactly you want—if you haven’t already done so, please take the five minutes or less it takes to fill out this survey and press the submit button.

Regardless of these changes and any others that result from your feedback, we know that you value our team’s expertise and independence, and those priorities—the bedrock of Robert Parker’s publication—won’t change. You’ll be able to continue to depend on our reviewers to call things as they see them, giving you the information you need to make your own wine-buying decisions.

Cheers,
Joe Czerwinski

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
I sent congratulations to Joe Czerwinski, who sent a nice reply. I’ve tasted with him about half a dozen times. He is a tough but fair judge of wines that are the most important part of my import portfolio. I think he is an excellent choice for this job.

I’ve never met William, but have corresponded with him and have great respect for his palate, judgment and writing ability. I see this as a positive move for the Wine Advocate.

For not only selfish reasons, I hope that Joe keeps the south of France as part of his remit. I think he has a great affinity for the region. It could make sense for the Wine Advocate to hire somebody separate to cover Australia and New Zealand (possibly adding South Africa). As Joe mentioned, they are actively looking for an additional reviewer (sorry, I am not available). There are an infinite number of ways to split up the responsibilities, but in today’s world it is truly daunting to ask somebody based in the U.S. to cover the south.

Dan Kravitz

I resubscribed to TWA today (after cancelling 12 years ago following the Jay Miller/Pancho Campo scandals and Parker’s assholery regarding their questionable ethics) solely because William Kelley will have a prominent role going forward. The website is even worse than it was during Parker’s day; I’m guessing that it was designed by the My Wines folks then ignored for 10 years. New management has its work cut out for it after a significant period of neglect and I wish them every success.

One factor that cannot be overlooked: William Kelley is more candid and less adversarial than his immediate predecessors and beyond (Parker, Galloni). This will serve him and his subscribers well. I hope.

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked:
I wonder if Cellartracker integration is a realistic possibility now. After listening to Levine on XChateau I doubt that he needs the advocate for the future, as opposed to the early days of development. He appears to be doing quite well on his own and has ambitious plans. They have a completely different approach, “Swiss Neutrality” in gathering data from users. Given some of the issues with WA in the past, why should he take his focus away from a crowd sourced trove of information?

That being said, I am also sorely tempted to re-subscribe to the Advocate to read more of WK’s content. Who knows, there might even be a temptation to include actual wine writing in the publication as opposed to endless tasting notes, scores, and reviews of some pretty marginal wine regions that most of us will never delve into. To me that was always the Achilles Heel of the Advocate; lack of more editorial opinion and back stories. I can get numbers anywhere these days. I wish LPB well, don’t think she was anything sinister, just probably in WAY over her head trying to replace the Pope of Monkton.

Cheers William! Good luck Joe!

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked:
… and reviews of some pretty marginal wine regions that most of us will never delve into.

…

I’m sorry Dennis, I have to strongly disagree on the marginal wine regions bit.

If there is one aspect that is a strength of Wine Advocate is the range of regions they cover.

The only other publication with that range is Wine Enthusiast, and those are the most suspect ratings out there for me. Wine Spectator is severely limited in regions. Others are one-person-shows and necessarily limited by that, or are region-only like MFW or JLL.

If a consumer wants to delve into new regions, they need either a guide or unlimited money. Most don’t have the latter.

After a consumer delves into an area, they’ll need pro opinions less and less. They can have their own. It’s at the outset where it matters most. I want to try wine from the Canary Islands but don’t want to take a flyer blind because, if I don’t like it, I won’t know if the problem is that I picked wrong or that the region is not for me. Those scores and notes help.

I’m not asking them to cover Perú or Bolivia (I’ve had wine from both and the Bolivian one was better but I went in blind). I would like them to cover Mexico (Guía Peñín is the only one that currently does that I’m aware of), and to cover Greece more and Germany more widely. And I’m sure there are more areas. Which regions that Wine Advocate covers would you cut?

Another reason to cover less prominent areas is vintage reports. There may be wall to wall coverage of the weather in Bordeaux, Burgundy, Champagne, Napa or even Piedmont but try finding the same for Santorini, Sicily, Bierzo, Rías Baixas. Sure, for the latter two, Spanish speakers could suscribe to Guía Peñín, but the same could be said about the French or Italian wine press. I guess consumers would have to come here and ask about Willamette or Santa Barbara.

Anyway, strong feelings on my part that regional breadth matters.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
GuillermoM:

Guaia Penin sells their physical paper annual in multiple languages, including English. I have not actually used their (free) TN website which seems to come with it though. I don’t know why, but copies can be had silly cheap on Amazon. Maybe they are surplus or something. Although they use a panel of professionals, my general impression of “Penin” is they tend to prefer bigger/plumper wines, which might happen to anyone forced taste hundreds of wines every day.

I actually take Dennis view on coverage of non collectible regions, though. For consumers in the US, there are a lot of filters that will keep out crazy/flawed/bad wines given our importing/distribution system. So the weird wacky $15 Canary Islands wine isn’t going to survive all the hurdles needed to get the bottle placed on a store shelf. (Alice will have to hand sell it to her cult/list as the ultimate pairing with papas arrugadas!) The obscure will have to stand on its price, value, and sponsorship which is why these tend not to be priced egregiously. There is no speculative element baked in the price.

But the pricey collectible regions, where bottles may run from hundreds to thousands, aren’t a segment where everyone can taste and try, and vote with their dollars. The critics do provide some value especially to speculators in that they help calibrate the prices, when most will be unable to assess that on their own for many years.

That’s not a space that I care about but I can understand the role played there, while championing obscura (like aforementioned Canary Islands) seems more apropos to an incented (and potentially penalized) player like a retailer/importer/distributor.

PS: I like regional specialist critical models better, especially if they are on the ground.

PPS: I like WB/BWE comments the best. I was hunting around last night trying to figure out the various relationships between various Borgogno family members last night, prior to clicking a ‘place order’ button.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked:
I lean more toward wanting coverage of more areas, especially if there’s value and deliciousness to be had. Priorat, Bierzo, and Ribeira Sacra were just as “obscure” to me 10 years ago as the Canaries are today and I’m glad we’ve had lots of coverage of them during that decade.

That said, can we start with more Bandol coverage? Hardly an obscure region, but seems to fall outside the scope of most reviewers - too far to make the drive from CdP, I guess? JLL doesn’t cover them at all (and didn’t even before he had to scale back), Claude reviews a few now and again, and Joe Cz has certainly covered more recently for TWA than anyone has for Vinous, but even on TWA, the last single-vineyard Tempier reds reviewed are the 2014s, and the last Pradeaux rouge reviewed is the 2005!

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked:
GuillermoM:

Guaia Penin sells their physical paper annual in multiple languages, including English. I have not actually used their (free) TN website which seems to come with it though. I don’t know why, but copies can be had silly cheap on Amazon. Maybe they are surplus or something. Although they use a panel of professionals, my general impression of “Penin” is they tend to prefer bigger/plumper wines, which might happen to anyone forced taste hundreds of wines every day.

Their free website only covers until the previous year’s tasting. For the current ones you have to pay.

As to their leanings I guess that could be true on reds. Not sure on whites which they cover for Spain more extensively than anyone else. Their notes in general aren’t very informative anyway. And, back to the reds, if you feel that way then you would agree that we benefit from others on the ground tasting too.

I actually take Dennis view on coverage of non collectible regions, though. For consumers in the US, there are a lot of filters that will keep out crazy/flawed/bad wines given our importing/distribution system. So the weird wacky $15 Canary Islands wine isn’t going to survive all the hurdles needed to get the bottle placed on a store shelf. (Alice will have to hand sell it to her cult/list as the ultimate pairing with papas arrugadas!)

I was walking my dog and burst out laughing on the rooftop.

Though she’d probably say patatas for the full effect.

The obscure will have to stand on its price, value, and sponsorship which is why these tend not to be priced egregiously. There is no speculative element baked in the price.

But the pricey collectible regions, where bottles may run from hundreds to thousands, aren’t a segment where everyone can taste and try, and vote with their dollars. The critics do provide some value especially to speculators in that they help calibrate the prices, when most will be unable to assess that on their own for many years.

That’s not a space that I care about but I can understand the role played there, while championing obscura (like aforementioned Canary Islands) seems more apropos to an incented (and potentially penalized) player like a retailer/importer/distributor.

As Dave said or implied, the difference between the obscure and the collectible is precisely the work of the critics.

And sure I like cheap prices as much as the next guy (believe me) but producers deserve value and recognition for their work, especially if breaking ground.

Given demand, the only way for Burgundy prices to normalize a bit is for Oregon and New Zealand to grow stronger and be more desirable and for more regions of sorts of wines too, so that demand becomes more diverse.

PS: I like regional specialist critical models better, especially if they are on the ground.

Yeah, regional specialists are great but, as John Gilman once pointed out (and I’m paraphrasing), regularly tasting German Rieslings is what helps him be a contrarian on some Bordeaux and notice more than specialists when acidity is lacking (and in some cases plummeting).

That’s one of the reasons I recommended whoever tastes Burgundy also taste some Oregon so we avoid self-reinforcing unwanted drift, and so consumers can see how the same palate views the different approaches.


PPS: I like WB/BWE comments the best. I was hunting around last night trying to figure out the various relationships between various Borgogno family members last night, prior to clicking a ‘place order’ button.

I like them too. But if we had the readership of the critics, and everyone was participating here, would you still feel the same way?

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked:
I lean more toward wanting coverage of more areas, especially if there’s value and deliciousness to be had. Priorat, Bierzo, and Ribeira Sacra were just as “obscure” to me 10 years ago as the Canaries are today and I’m glad we’ve had lots of coverage of them during that decade.

That said, can we start with more Bandol coverage? Hardly an obscure region, but seems to fall outside the scope of most reviewers - too far to make the drive from CdP, I guess? JLL doesn’t cover them at all (and didn’t even before he had to scale back), Claude reviews a few now and again, and Joe Cz has certainly covered more recently for TWA than anyone has for Vinous, but even on TWA, the last single-vineyard Tempier reds reviewed are the 2014s, and the last Pradeaux rouge reviewed is the 2005!

Like I said, I agree.

Wanted to add that WS does cover Bandol a bit more. Tons of rosés. Some 2018 and 2017 reds.

This month, Molesworth gave 93 pts to the 2018 Château de Pibarnon, saying it “almost gushes with warm cassis and plum puree flavors, laced with hints of violet, red tea, worn leather and dried rosemary. … Textbook Bandol. “

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked:
Wanted to add that WS does cover Bandol a bit more. Tons of rosés. Some 2018 and 2017 reds.

This month, Molesworth gave 93 pts to the 2018 Château de Pibarnon, saying it “almost gushes with warm cassis and plum puree flavors, laced with hints of violet, red tea, worn leather and dried rosemary. … Textbook Bandol. “

Joe Cz did review 38 rosés from 2018, so hopefully that’s a sign of things to come and the red coverage will similarly expand from where it had been. I haven’t looked at WS recently - glad to hear they are doing the region justice.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Thanks again, everyone! We have a lot to think about and you’ll definitely see some changes. Just give us a bit of time to get the ball rolling. And in the meantime, I encourage anyone who is a subscriber to fill out the survey to give their opinion. Obviously, Joe and I have a strong sense of the direction we want to take. But we absolutely want to know as much as possible about our subscribers’ priorities!

posts.name: Brent Clayton
posts.cooked:
Lisa takes one final shot at Jeb on the way out…

The Drinks Business – 1 Dec 21

Lisa Perrotti-Brown MW to leave The Wine Advocate - The Drinks Business

Lisa Perrotti-Brown MW is to leave the Robert Parker’s Wine Advocate later this month after 13 years at the journal, telling db that “it felt like time to do something else.”

Est. reading time: 2 minutes

link_counts.title: Lisa Perrotti-Brown MW to leave The Wine Advocate - The Drinks Business

posts.name: Max Swomley
posts.cooked:
Thanks again, everyone! We have a lot to think about and you’ll definitely see some changes. Just give us a bit of time to get the ball rolling. And in the meantime, I encourage anyone who is a subscriber to fill out the survey to give their opinion. Obviously, Joe and I have a strong sense of the direction we want to take. But we absolutely want to know as much as possible about our subscribers’ priorities!

Any way to take the survey if we’ve just renewed our subscription?

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I wonder if Cellartracker integration is a realistic possibility now. After listening to Levine on XChateau I doubt that he needs the advocate for the future, as opposed to the early days of development. He appears to be doing quite well on his own and has ambitious plans. They have a completely different approach, “Swiss Neutrality” in gathering data from users. Given some of the issues with WA in the past, why should he take his focus away from a crowd sourced trove of information?

Thanks Dennis, for those interesting thoughts. I agree that things have rather changed for TWA and CT since the latter started, but since CT is already integrated with most other publications, I don’t really see why there would be an issue with TWA. None of the others affects CT’s “focus” in any way - the integration of their notes and scores is just a very useful service for those among their subscribers who happen to use CT. Personally I think it would be win-win: TWA would provide their subscribers with a much overdue service that has been requested ever since I can remember; CT would become an even more attractive proposition than it is already.
Actually, CT would provide a far clearer, more accessible service for TWA subscribers than the one they have had up to now. Trawling through notes on the TWA site is not exactly user-friendly. Also, I’m not obsessed with critics’ notes and so forth, but it would be fun to be able to compare write-ups directly on the wine’s page.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
I wonder if Cellartracker integration is a realistic possibility now. After listening to Levine on XChateau I doubt that he needs the advocate for the future, as opposed to the early days of development. He appears to be doing quite well on his own and has ambitious plans. They have a completely different approach, “Swiss Neutrality” in gathering data from users. Given some of the issues with WA in the past, why should he take his focus away from a crowd sourced trove of information?

Maybe my view is too simplistic but it’s that Eric has always been focused on providing customer value and convenient access to information. Linking to pro notes that a user has already paid for doesn’t take focus away from what’s already in CT. It just makes CT more powerful in allowing the user to access the information they want.

What’s a mystery to me is why WA would not want this integration.

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked:
Interesting thoughts…I am not a CT user so I have no dog in this fight. I guess I prefer the Higgeldy Piggity system of organizing my cellar in an idiosyncratic way that does not rely on any technology. But then again I don’t store anything off site, I can see where this may be very useful for those who must have storage and cannot rummage around the basement to find out what wines they have forgotten about over the last 20 years.

(Did I really drink that Mugneret Gibourg 2014 NSG or did I just move it again? What Idiot bought this old James Irvine Aussie Merlot? Oh yeah, that idiot was me…)

I suppose the ultimate paranoid reason (OK I admit it) is that I hate to have a comprehensive listing of all my wines listed on an internet platform that the IRS could potentially access in the future. (So Mr Borczon, how is it on an income of $XXXXX.XX you were able to afford multiple vintages of Clos du Tart and your wife is still alive?) After all isn’t Biden floating the idea of accessing bank records with transactions over $600.00? I don’t want my Cellartracker records subpoenaed some day

With or without Cellartracker, I am going to re-subscribe to the WA. Personally I would like to have this board replace the Parker Board officially so I could have the fun of throwing stones internally at the reviewers of the Advocate and expect them to respond!

PS. How much exactly is Michelin paying William if he can afford to be drinking '21 Margaux? I thought all wine writers were supposed to be poor…

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: FYI whether you use CT to track your cellar, the whole point of the integration is so when you are browsing tasting notes and prices on a wine you are looking at purchasing, you can see RP ratings and points right there just like you currently can with many of the other critics. It saves the time of separately searching RP.com.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
PS. How much exactly is Michelin paying William if he can afford to be drinking '21 Margaux? I thought all wine writers were supposed to be poor…

I don’t understand the intention behind this comment. How do any of us know how or why people have access to the wines they do, including wine writers, and why should we care? Other than implications of buying scores, which clearly isn’t the case here, I can’t come up with a reason. I, for one, am delighted that William has the opportunity to drink wines like this and share his insights so I can learn.

posts.name: Eric LeVine
posts.cooked:
I wonder if Cellartracker integration is a realistic possibility now. After listening to Levine on XChateau I doubt that he needs the advocate for the future, as opposed to the early days of development. He appears to be doing quite well on his own and has ambitious plans. They have a completely different approach, “Swiss Neutrality” in gathering data from users. Given some of the issues with WA in the past, why should he take his focus away from a crowd sourced trove of information?

Thanks Dennis, for those interesting thoughts. I agree that things have rather changed for TWA and CT since the latter started, but since CT is already integrated with most other publications, I don’t really see why there would be an issue with TWA. None of the others affects CT’s “focus” in any way - the integration of their notes and scores is just a very useful service for those among their subscribers who happen to use CT. Personally I think it would be win-win: TWA would provide their subscribers with a much overdue service that has been requested ever since I can remember; CT would become an even more attractive proposition than it is already.
Actually, CT would provide a far clearer, more accessible service for TWA subscribers than the one they have had up to now. Trawling through notes on the TWA site is not exactly user-friendly. Also, I’m not obsessed with critics’ notes and so forth, but it would be fun to be able to compare write-ups directly on the wine’s page.

Every few years, going on 18 years now, I try to start up conversations with the TWA to do integration. The last time we did this was with the new CEO, just before COVID started. There were a couple of conversations and then radio silence. And looking my my calendar, there were some conversations with previous management back in 2018.

I am here. Always willing to collaborate. This is in everyone’s interest.

posts.name: Vinod Stalam
posts.cooked: Great to hear William is getting additional coverage and responsibility in a classic wine region. I will find it fascinating to see how he covers the additional space, since he’s been a real eye opener for me in Beaujolais and areas of white burgundy off the beaten track that are still reasonably affordable (at least in the UK). I hope he can cover similar ground for Bordeaux, since although I’m happy for anyone that can source premier and grand crus from 50 yrs ago and go deep on vintage variation for 1st growth bordeaux, it’s just relevant for 99% of wine consumers and certainly those born within 20 years of William.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Great to hear William is getting additional coverage and responsibility in a classic wine region. I will find it fascinating to see how he covers the additional space, since he’s been a real eye opener for me in Beaujolais and areas of white burgundy off the beaten track that are still reasonably affordable (at least in the UK). I hope he can cover similar ground for Bordeaux, since although I’m happy for anyone that can source premier and grand crus from 50 yrs ago and go deep on vintage variation for 1st growth bordeaux, it’s just relevant for 99% of wine consumers and certainly those born within 20 years of William.

Don’t worry, it’s part of the plan!

It’s also going to be interesting to shed some light on contemporary Bordelais viticulture. I spend Saturday morning pruning in Beaune with Michel Duclos, one of France’s leading pruning consultants, and Michel Falquier, vineyard manger for the Mouiex estates. Very stimulating discussing differences between the Médoc and Right Bank, as well as of course differences between Bordeaux and Burgundy.

posts.name: R 1 c k  D y 3 r
posts.cooked:
Lisa Perrotti-Brown has never been popular, but for those of us who subscribed to Robert Parker because we liked his approach, I think her reviewing work and scores reflected the same preferences for big, bold wines. She provided consistency when she took over in terms of the types of wines that garnered high scores, providing some continuity in the editorial outlook by bringing the same type of “lens” to viewing the wine regions she covered.

I’ve been less clear about her overall editorial leadership and running of the business (the site is a dog and these side hustles like “Matter of Taste” do nothing for me) , but I think her work as a critic deserves more credit than people seem to give her. That said, tastes change over time and I’ve increasingly found my palate diverging from the Parker mold. I’ve been thinking about dropping my RP subscription for some time but will now hold off and see how things go under a new editorial banner.

From my standpoint, I enjoy subscribing to professional reviewers with different viewpoint. The Bordeaux VN reviews from Neal Martin and Galloni have been fascinating in that regard. I wish there was an easier way for critics to provide an outline of their “philosophy” of what they look for in a wine. Instead, it always seems to require following their reviews over time and comparing to your own palate. So when a new reviewer takes over (which has happened in recent years at RP), it’s hard for their scores and writings to be understood in the context of your own palate.

Thinking about it, as I increasingly rely as much on notes from “the rabble” on Cellar Tracker as the professional reviewers to which I subscribe (WS, WA, and VN), I wish there was a way you could add a few sentences on your CT profile so that people could easily hover over and get a perspective of your outlook when checking out your notes.

You can set a public profile easily on CT which is actually just 1-click away. Just go here and set it: https://www.cellartracker.com/editprofile.asp

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I wish there was an easier way for critics to provide an outline of their “philosophy” of what they look for in a wine. Instead, it always seems to require following their reviews over time and comparing to your own palate. So when a new reviewer takes over (which has happened in recent years at RP), it’s hard for their scores and writings to be understood in the context of your own palate.

I have felt this myself. So, to help readers make sense of where I stand, I’m going to publish 50 or so tasting notes on recently enjoyed more or less mature Bordeaux on the site early next year. I hope that will help you get a real feel for my palate, as opposed to any sort of generic statement of my stylistic preferences.
